coredata.dc:title: Highly selective fluorescence sensor sensing benzo[a]pyrene in water utilizing carbon dots derived from 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yi
Zhou, Qingxiang
Sheng, Xueying
Li, Shuanying
Tong, Yayan
Guo, Jinghan
Zhou, Boyao
Zhao, Jingyi
Liu, Menghua
Li, Zhi
Li, Yanhui
Qu, Tongxu
Chen, Chunmao
coredata.dc:description: 4-Carboxyphenylboronic acid was used as the single precursor to facilely prepare fluorescent carbon quantum dots by one-step solvothermal method. The as-obtained carbon dots (CDs) exhibited highly selective and sensitive for benzo[a]pyrene (BaP), and may be a splendid sensor for sensing BaP. The principle was that the as-prepared CDs could form a complex with BaP through hydrophobic interaction which causes the decrease of fluorescence intensity of CDs by static quenching principle. The constructed ﬂuorescent sensor exhibited excellent linearity ranged from 0.002 to 0.06 μg mL−1 and provided a low limit of detection of 0.16 ng mL−1. The experimental results showed that this ﬂuorescent sensor resulted in simplicity, rapidness, low cost, short analytical time, and high sensitivity and stability. Validation with real water samples endowed the sensor high reliability and feasibility for BaP determination in practical application in various samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensor
Benzo[a]pyrene
Fluorescence quenching

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of as-obtained CDs. (a) TEM images of the CDs. Inset: size distribution histogram. (b) Fluorescent spectra at various excitation wavelengths. (c) UV–vis spectrum of BaP (1 μg mL−1) and the CDs (10 μg mL−1), emission spectrum of the CDs. (d) FT-IR spectra of the CDs and 4-CPBA. (e) Fluorescent spectra of CDs before and after adding BaP at 50 ng mL−1.

Optimization of parameters. (a) Effect pH on synthesis of CDs. (b) Effect of heating time on synthesis of CDs. (c) Effect of CDs concentration on detection. (d) Effect of pH value on detection. (e) Effect of NaCl on ΔF between CDs and BaP. (f) Effect of incubation time.
(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs and BaP under different conditions. (b) Zeta potentials of the CDs, CDs with BaP under the same condition.
(a) Fluorescence quenching of CDs with various levels of BaP. (b) Linear plots based on ﬂuorescence intensity change vs concentration of BaP.
Selectivity of the fluorescence sensor for sensing BaP.
Illustration of the as-obtained CDs and its sensing principle for BaP.
Comparison of different methods with proposed sensor for the analysis of BaP.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SERS, Surface enhanced Raman spectrometry; e-MIPs-HPLC-UV, electrochemical molecular imprinted polymers-high performance liquid chromatography-ultraviolet detector; GPC-GC-MS, Gel permeation chromatography-gas chromatography-mass spectrometry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spiked recoveries for BaP from real water samples.

head.ce:title: Highly selective fluorescence sensor sensing benzo[a]pyrene in water utilizing carbon dots derived from 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, State Key Laboratory of Petroleum Pollution Control, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
State Key Laboratory of Petroleum Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, State Key Laboratory of Petroleum Pollution Control, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing, 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 4-Carboxyphenylboronic acid was used as the single precursor to facilely prepare fluorescent carbon quantum dots by one-step solvothermal method. The as-obtained carbon dots (CDs) exhibited highly selective and sensitive for benzo[a]pyrene (BaP), and may be a splendid sensor for sensing BaP. The principle was that the as-prepared CDs could form a complex with BaP through hydrophobic interaction which causes the decrease of fluorescence intensity of CDs by static quenching principle. The constructed ﬂuorescent sensor exhibited excellent linearity ranged from 0.002 to 0.06 μg mL−1 and provided a low limit of detection of 0.16 ng mL−1. The experimental results showed that this ﬂuorescent sensor resulted in simplicity, rapidness, low cost, short analytical time, and high sensitivity and stability. Validation with real water samples endowed the sensor high reliability and feasibility for BaP determination in practical application in various samples.

ce:section.ce:para: The pollution of organic compounds in water causes many adverse effects on human health. Since benzo[a]pyrene (BaP) was firstly isolated from coal tar and research proved that BaP could induce skin cancer in mice, BaP became an important environmental chemical carcinogen discovered (Kim et al., 2013). Meanwhile, because of its structure, physical-chemical properties and large consumption, BaP is relatively stable, and widely exposed to air, soil and water, which easily pollutes environment and foods. BaP can not only enter into the human body directly through the respiratory tract and skin, but also enters human body indirectly through the food chains, so BaP is an important substance owing to its toxicity caused by carcinogenicity, teratogenicity and certain damage to the immune system and reproductive system (Butler et al., 1993; Fang et al., 2020). Since then, it is of great significance to take effective control measures to reduce its hazards and to establish rapid and effective monitoring methods for BaP.

There are many challenges in the process of detecting BaP. First, the environmental matrix is more complicated, and BaP is present in environment at trace level, which retains some impurity components interfering with the detection of BaP. Hence, it will inevitably result in certain pretreatment for obtaining accurate analytical results, for example, in order to achieve good analytical performance, a magnetic solid phase extraction with fatty acid modified Fe3O4, solid phase extraction with gold nanoparticles or conventional liquid-liquid extraction procedure was carried out before analysis on high-performance liquid chromatography and fluorescence detection (HPLC–FLD), laser-excited time-resolved Shpol'skii spectrometry, or Surface Enhanced Raman Spectrometry(SERS) (Liao et al., 2015; Wang and Campiglia, 2010; Zhou et al., 2020). Second, more compounds with similar structure of BaP bring greater difficulty to the separation process (Kafouris et al., 2020). In response to these challenges, a simple method should be employed to reduce the loss of BaP resulted from the sample pretreatment step as much as possible. In addition, some advanced and highly selective separation techniques should be combined to avoid the interference of similar substances on the analytical results. Nowadays researchers have paid much attention on facile detection of BaP, and there have been some valuable methods which are based on liquid chromatography and gas chromatography with various specific detectors, and electrochemistry (Ni et al., 2014; Olatunji et al., 2014; Tseng et al., 2014; Dalvand and Ghiasvand, 2019; Akvan et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2020), etc. However, the above-mentioned methods mostly need complicated and strict requirements or expensive analytical instruments for sample pretreatment and analytical detection which limited their practical applications.

In contrast, fluorescent method is a very facile and rapid method for BaP detection, which was endowed with more benefits such as great sensitivity, knockdown detection limit and good selectivity. Nowadays, the appearance of new fluorescent nanomaterials endows fluorescent method new vitality and wide application prospects. More and more research interests have been focused on fabrication and application of fluorescent nanomaterials especially the functionalized materials like carbon dots, quantum dots, metal nanoparticles, nanotubes, etc. Among them, Carbon dots (CDs) have many outstanding properties compared to other fluorescent materials, including facilely fabrication, fluorescent stability, excellent solubility in water, hypotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, which endowed them wide applications in different fields such as sensing, photocatalysis, bioimaging, drug delivery and optoelectronic devices (Ding et al., 2015; Hou et al., 2016; Kuo et al., 2016; Shahid et al., 2020; Siafaka et al., 2021). Up to now, many carbon dots from different carbon sources have been prepared and which have utilized successfully to construct excellent fluorescence sensors measuring metal ions and organic pollutants according to various mechanisms. Moreover, the size of CDs, the constituent elements and the fluorescence characteristics of CDs can be adjusted through different synthesis methods (Kurian and Paul, 2021). Various synthesis technologies have been developed to prepare fluorescent CDs, such as combustion, arc discharge technology, hydrothermal treatment, electrochemical oxidation, acidic oxidation, laser ablation and microwave-assisted methods (Doñate-Buendía et al., 2020; Hou et al., 2015, 2016,; Li et al., 2011; Hu et al., 2014; Hao et al., 2015; Castro et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2016a; Muthusankar et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2021), and so on. Among them, hydrothermal method is the most common and simplest synthesis method, and which is most widely used.

CDs are effective fluorescence nanoprobes for detecting organic pollutants in the environment. CDs have received more attention as ‘‘off-on’’ sensors. In the “off-on” sensing platforms, CDs can directly undergo fluorescence increase or quenching with a rapid response and simple operation after adding the target pollutant (Alvandi et al., 2021). Zhu et al. established a ratonmetric fluorescent probe with carbon dots from plum for the detection of anti-tumor antibiotic doxorubicin (DOX) with a wide linear range. This probe has good anti-interference and has been successfully applied to the quantitative analysis of DOX in human urine and human serum. (Zhu et al., 2021). Our group has also prepared several useful CDs with sodium citrate and polyacrylamide and chocolate, which were successfully employed to fabricate extra-sensitive chemo-sensors for measuring lead, pyrophosphate and pH (Liu et al., 2016b, 2017a, 2017b; Liu and Zhou (2017)). In general, the fluorescent fluorescence sensor is often constructed owing to change of fluorescence intensity of CDs derived from which caused by the interaction between the target and CDs. The fluorescence quenching results from different mechanisms such as Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter eﬀect (IFE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and static quenching effect (SQE) (Bao et al., 2021; Gu and Kenny, 2009), etc.

Herein, the CDs is prepared with 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid as carbon precursor by a facile solvothermal method, and which is investigated to be an effective sensor to monitor BaP. BaP could be absorbed onto the surface of carbon nanodots by intermolecular electrostatic interactions which will quench the fluorescence intensity of the as-obtained CDs. The synthetic process of CDs and the fluorescence quenching mechanism was depicted in Scheme 1 .

4-Carboxyphenylboronic acid (4-CPBA, 97%) was provided by Aladdin Industrial Co. Ltd. Benzo[a]pyrene (BaP, 96%) was bought from Rhawn Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. Methanol and HCl were obtained from Beijing Chemical Works. NaOH, NaCl and other reagents were of analytical purity.
The instruments for characterization and determination was as the same as reported in our previous works (Liu et al., 2017a; Wang et al., 2019).

The CDs were prepared by an effectively modiﬁed one-pot hydrothermal method (Shen and Xia., 2014). Simply, 0.2 g 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid was mixed with ultrapure water (20 mL), and the pH of this mixture was adjusted to pH 3.0 with 0.1 M HCl under stirring. Then the mixture was bubbled with nitrogen gas to wipe out dissolved oxygen in solution for 1 h. In the end, the mixture solution was put into a Teﬂon-lined autoclave and kept heating at 160 °C for 8 h. Cooled to the ambient temperature, the solution with CDs was separated by centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for half an hour in order to wipe off large precipitates. A dialysis bag with molecular weight cutoﬀ of 1000 Da was used to purify CDs. The obtained CDs were kept in a refrigerator for use.

25 μL of CDs solution of 400 μg mL−1 and a certain amount of BaP was taken into a centrifuge tube. The solution was attenuated to 1.0 mL with ultrapure water. In the same way, BaP solution in the mixture was replaced with the same volume of methanol for the fluorescence detection to measure the reduction of fluorescence intensity. The excitation and emission wavelengths were fixed at 270 nm and 360 nm, respectively. Both slit widths were 5 nm.

For evaluating the reliability and applicability of the fluorescence sensor, four water samples were sampled and kept for analysis. These samples were collected from Jishuitan Lake, Binhe Park, Qingnian Lake and Shichahai Lake in Beijing, China. The collected water samples were pretreated with 0.22 μm filter membrane before analysis.

As exhibited from Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b, the TEM images of as-obtained CDs illustrated that prepared CDs were dispersed well and the shape of them was nearly spherical. The size distribution histogram showed that diameter of the obtained CDs was between 2.1 and 3.3 nm, whereas most CDs were equal to or near 3.0 nm. To further investigate the luminous characteristics of as-obtained CDs, fluorescent spectra of as-obtained CDs was recorded at various excitation wavelengths (Fig. 1c). It showed that the strongest emission at 360 nm when the optimal excitation wavelength was set at 270 nm. Fig. 1d shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of BaP and the obtained CDs. The UV spectrum of BaP overlaps with the emission spectrum of CDs, which may cause the fluorescence decrease due to IFE (Gu and Kenny, 2009). FT-IR spectrum of the obtained CDs (red curve, Fig. 1e) illustrated apparent absorption at 1105, 1138, 1295 and 1397 cm−1. They apparently demonstrated the B–O–H deformation vibration, C–B stretching vibration, B–O–H bending vibration, and B–O stretching vibration, respectively. The strong absorption bands at 1597 cm−1 and 3444 cm−1 might be attributed to the in-plane bending and stretching vibrations of O–H (Shen and Xia, 2014). The FT-IR result showed that boronic acid groups and carboxyl groups had been transferred from 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid to the CDs surface successfully. Fig. 1f demonstrates that the ﬂuorescence quenching of the CDs with introduction of BaP was remarkable.

It is found that BaP can effectively inhibit the fluorescence intensity of CDs, which exhibits that the as-obtained CDs may be used to develop a fluorescence sensor to detect BaP (Fig. 1f). The essential factors including the parameters for the fabrication of the CDs, the concentration of CDs as well as the pH value of the mixed solution were further investigated to achieve the best parameters for detecting BaP. During the investigation process, BaP was fortified at 50 ng mL−1. The fluorescent intensity change value (F0–F, ΔF) was utilized as the fluorescence quenching signal, where F0 was the fluorescent intensity of CDs without BaP and F was the fluorescent intensity with BaP. Fig. 2 a and Fig. 2b exhibits the impact of pH and heating time on the synthesis of CDs with solvothermal method. Apparently, the fluorescent intensity change (ΔF) of the as-obtained CDs enhanced with the increase of pH value from pH 1.0 to pH 3.0, and then significantly decreased with continuous increase of pH value, which indicated that the CDs obtained at pH 3.0 achieved the strongest quenching effect. The synthetic time played an important role in achieving the best CDs. In the process of hydrothermal synthesis of CDs, temperature have a significant impact on the fluorescence characteristics of CDs, so the heating temperature needs to be optimized. The results demonstrated that the ΔF also enhanced along-with prolonging of heating time up to 8 h, and dropped with further increase of heating time. Hence, CDs obtained at 8 h resulted in the best quenching performance.

It's obvious that the sensor can show a higher sensitivity when the concentration of CDs is lower, whereas the concentration of CDs is higher, the linear range of the sensor can be broader, it is critical to choose a suitable CDs concentration. As shown in Fig. 2c, the ΔF has an increase accompanied by rise of the concentration of CDs with its concentration lower than 10 μg mL−1, and falls down slowly with its concentration higher than 10 μg mL−1. The unideal fluorescence quenching effect may be due to the insufficient amount of the reactants if the amount of CDs was too low. Similarly, spontaneous self-absorption phenomenon may arise if the concentration of CDs was too high which may affect the absorbance of the solution. Finally, CDs was fortified at 10 μg mL−1.

Fig. 2d exhibits the impact of pH value on ΔF in the analytical process. It was obvious that ΔF increased gradually with pH rising to pH 3.0, then declined with pH continuously rising over pH 3.0. It was considered that the activity of CDs surface was affected with the change of pH, which may be due to the fact that CDs earns similar pKa with 4-CPBA of about 4.0. When the solution pH was 3.0, the CDs were near electric neutrality, and which resulted in the strongest interaction between CDs and BaP causing the largest ΔF value. When the solution was strong acidic or basic, the CDs may have positive or negative charge and which resulted in strong electrostatic repulsion and weakened the quenching effect. Hence, pH 3.0 was the optimal pH for the determination of BaP.

Fig. 2e indicates that ionic strength has almost no influence on fluorescence intensity in the measurement process of BaP with CDs. Since the salt concentration in seawater is about 3.5%, this chemosensor remains stable in a high-salt system, which provides an effective method for the detection of BaP under physiological and more severe conditions. Meanwhile, the incubation time is also a remarkable factor that will affect the measurement of BaP with CDs, which is optimized over the range of 1–30 min. As shown in Fig. 2f, different incubation times have different effect on the fluorescence quenching. It showed that ΔF value had fluctuated slightly in this detection system within 10 min, and then had no significant change, which demonstrated that the interaction of BaP and CDs was quite rapid and robust. These results indicated that the fluorescence sensor was rapid and stable in determining BaP, which endowed this sensor valuable prospects for the practical application.

Generally, the interactions between target analyte with the quencher are multitudinous including electron or energy transfer, collisional quenching, excited-state reaction, and ground-state complex formation and so on, which will decline the fluorescent quantum yield. The quenching principles are commonly split into two typical kinds of dynamic quenching and static quenching. Dynamic quenching is due to collision, and static quenching is often owing to forming a ground-state complex between the analyte and the quencher (Fu et al., 2021). Dynamic quenching refers to the collision of the fluorescent substance molecules in the solution with the quencher molecules, and the former loses energy and causes the fluorescence intensity to decrease. It can also be called collision quenching, which can be verified by carrying out temperature comparison experiments. The higher the temperature, which means the collision is violent, and the fluorescence intensity decreases more obviously. Generally, the collision quenching constantly increases when temperature rises. Static quenching, because a part of fluorescent substance molecules and the quencher can form a non-fluorescent complex, which can also be called component compound quenching, which can be verified by UV absorption spectroscopy. The formation of a new compound may cause the UV absorption peak position to shift, or a new absorption peak may appear, and an isoabsorption point may appear.

As shown from Fig. 1c, the emission spectrum of the prepared CDs covered part of the absorption spectrum of BaP, and that may cause the fluorescence quenching. Considering the spectra were highly overlapped, the probable mechanism of the quenching could be IFE or FRET (Dong et al., 2012). In order to further explore its mechanism, the UV spectra of CDs, BaP and CDs-BaP mixture were recorded. After adding BaP, the absorption spectra of CDs-BaP mixture was significantly different from the sum value of BaP and CDs when the wavelength was over than 250 nm (Fig. 3 a). Based on these results, the decline of fluorescent intensity of CDs was not due to IFE and CDs-BaP complex was probably formed (Yan et al., 2014, 2016).

In order to clarify the quenching mechanism for the measurement of BaP, the zeta potential of the CDs were investigated. The results showed that the zeta potential of the CDs migrated from −5.54 to −0.945 mV with addition of BaP, which indirectly supported the presence of static quenching between BaP and CDs (Fig. 3b). It is considered that the molecular distance between BaP and CDs may be reduced due to electrostatic attraction, which may cause the formation of complex and fluorescence quenching of CDs (Purbia and Paria, 2016).

To further evaluate the reliability of this fluorescence sensor, the inter-day and intra-day precisions were investigated in detail. BaP was spiked at 0.1, 2.5 and 10 ng mL−1 (n = 6) in the experiments. The results showed that the inter-day precisions for BaP levels of 0.1, 2.5 and 10 ng mL−1 were 0.48, 1.05, and 0.06% (RSD, n = 6), and the intra-day precisions for BaP levels of 0.1, 2.5 and 10 ng mL−1 were 1.26, 0.88, and 0.12% (RSD, n = 6), respectively.

For a fluorescence sensor, it also needs a high selectivity in addition to high sensitivity. In this experiment, four polycylic aromatic hydrocarbon similar in structure to BaP including anthracene, phenanthrene, pyrene and fluoranthene were utilized to investigate its selectivity. Firstly, the above-mentioned interference substances were mixed individually with BaP and the CDs, and observe the respective fluorescence quenching effects under the optimal conditions. The results were depicted in Fig. 5 . It was clear that the fluorescence of the sensor was significantly quenched (blue bar); when other PAHs were added, the fluorescent intensity of the sensor had no marked change as shown in Fig. 5 (red bar). These results illuminated that these interference substances did not have salient influence on the fluorescence quenching of BaP, which meant that these substances with similar structure had no effect on the determination of BaP with this sensor. All the results indicated that this sensor has good selectivity for BaP detection.

For validating the reliability, possibility and applicability of the fluorescence sensor in practical application, four water samples were collected from Jishuitan Lake, Binhe Park, Qingnian Lake and Shicha Lake in Beijing, China. At the same time, the waste water from an asphalt plant of a Petrochemical Co. Ltd. in North China was collected for evaluating the proposed sensor. The blank and spiked samples at 5, 12.5 and 50 μg L−1 were determined. The analytical data were illustrated in Table 2 . The sample recoveries ranged from 89.4 to 102.8% and the precisions were less than 4.3%. We also used GC-MS to determine the concentration of BaP in the wastewater sample of the asphalt plant to compare with proposed sensor. The concentration of BaP was 4.197 μg L−1 and basically consistent with the result from proposed sensor, which further proved the reliability of the proposed sensor. These results demonstrated that the proposed fluorescence sensor earned excellent sensitivity and stability for BaP measurement, and would be a useful alternative tool in future applications.

In this study, CDs were synthesized with 4-carboxyphenylboronic acid by a one-pot hydrothermal method and which was susceptive to BaP. The obtained CDs can be employed as an effective and sensitive ﬂuorescent sensor for monitoring BaP in water. The possible mechanism of CDs sensor was due to forming of CDs-BaP complex. The sensor provided excellent linear range of 0.002–0.06 μg mL−1 and the detection limit was 0.16 ng mL−1. The research results indicated that the sensor showed many good characteristics such as facileness, speedy, and sensitivity. Real water samples analysis verified its reliability and applicability. The specific characteristics endows the ﬂuorescent sensor with a wonderful application potential in the future.

Yi Sun: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Qingxiang Zhou: Supervision, Software, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Xueying Sheng: Software, Data curation. Shuangying Li: Software, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Yayan Tong: Visualization, Investigation. Jinghan Guo: Visualization, Investigation. Boyao Zhou: Visualization, Investigation. Jingyi Zhao: Software, Data curation. Menghua Liu: Visualization, Investigation. Zhi Li: Software, Data curation. Yanhui Li: Software, Data curation.Tongxu Qu: Visualization, Investigation. Chunmao Chen: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21976211) and Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum, Beijing (No.2462020XKJS04).
